title: Culling a chicken humanely
subtitle: The two methods available to a smallholder for dispatching a bird quickly and cleanly.
excerpt: A cull is sometimes necessary: a bird that cannot recover from injury or illness, an older hen beyond her laying years, or a cockerel surplus to requirements. Knowing how to do this quickly and correctly is part of keeping chickens.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007. RSPCA guidance on humane slaughter of poultry. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The method of killing a bird by rapidly separating the vertebrae at the base of the skull from the neck, severing the spinal cord. When done correctly, it is instantaneous. It is a permitted on-farm dispatch method in the UK for poultry under the Welfare of Farmed Animals Regulations.

Rendering a bird unconscious by a firm, accurate blow to the skull. Used before killing in some commercial contexts; on a smallholding scale, cervical dislocation or a sharp knife are the most practical methods.

content.text: This tutorial describes the two on-farm dispatch methods available to a UK smallholder under the Welfare of Farmed Animals (England) Regulations 2007:

and the use of a killing cone with a sharp knife. Both are legal and both are painless when performed correctly. A slow, uncertain kill is what causes suffering; preparation and decisiveness prevent it.
You may dispatch your own birds on your own premises without a licence, by cervical dislocation for chickens, turkeys, ducks, and geese up to 70 days old, and by
for any size bird. The meat from birds killed on-farm without an abattoir inspection cannot be sold; it can be used for your own household consumption.

Catch the bird calmly. Hold her by the legs with your dominant hand. With the other hand, place two fingers around the back of her skull, thumb under the beak. In one firm, continuous movement, stretch the neck downward and backward, rotating the head away from you. The correct technique extends the neck and dislocates the atlas from the skull in one motion; there is an audible pop or click when it is done correctly. The wings will beat reflexively for several seconds; this is muscle activity after death, not distress.

Practice the positioning on a dead bird before attempting on a live one if you have not done this before. The common errors are: not stretching far enough, stopping the motion before the dislocation, or rotating without simultaneously stretching. Any of these results in an incomplete kill.
Using a killing cone and knife

A killing cone holds the bird inverted, keeping the wings still. Insert the bird headfirst into the cone with the head protruding below. The cone calms the bird within a few seconds. Use a sharp knife to make a single clean cut across the throat just below the jaw, severing both jugular veins and the carotid arteries. The bird loses consciousness immediately. Allow the carcass to bleed out fully before processing.

attrs.title: Confirming the kill
attrs.body: After cervical dislocation, check by looking at the eyes: a correctly killed bird has fixed, dilated pupils within 30 seconds and the neck hangs freely. Wing beating and paddling are reflexive and may continue for a minute; do not confuse this with signs of life. After the knife method, the bird is unconscious within seconds of the cut; ensure both vessels are completely severed.

content.text: Pluck and process the carcass within an hour of the kill, while the body is still warm and the feathers release more easily. If you are culling a sick bird, double-bag the carcass and dispose of it as household waste rather than adding it to the compost.
